ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers smoked fish broadly; no species-specific smoked salmon code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for smoked salmon as a food product in the publicly available SNOMED CT browser.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of smoked salmon is approximately 50g, equivalent to two to three thin slices, commonly served on bagels, crackers, or as part of a platter.

nutrient_source.title: Smoked Salmon — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database (CR ID: F03040012); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB No. 15236 (Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, smoked) for fatty acid and micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for smoked salmon (CR ID: F03040012), an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat (2.3g), monounsaturated fat (4.7g), polyunsaturated fat (2.4g), trans fat (0.0g), cholesterol (23mg), and potassium (330mg) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB No. 15236 (Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, smoked), an approved secondary global source, as the HPB entry does not report fatty acid breakdown or potassium. Both sources are on the approved source list per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md.

gi_source.title: N/A — Smoked salmon contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Smoked salmon contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g as confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo (CR ID: F03040012). Glycemic index is not applicable for this food as it contains negligible carbohydrates. Per Project Veritas v2 critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI database lookup is required or appropriate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (1015mg/100g), far exceeding the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Smoked salmon should be strictly limited or avoided by individuals with hypertension; sodium at 1015mg/100g represents over 40% of the recommended daily sodium limit (2400mg) in a single 100g serving, and even a 50g portion contributes approximately 508mg sodium, making portion control essential and fresh unsalted salmon strongly preferred.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content with high protein (24.3g/100g) and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
type2_diabetes.guidance: Smoked salmon is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains no carbohydrates or sugars and therefore has no glycaemic impact; however, the high sodium content (1015mg/100g) warrants portion moderation, and sweet-cured or honey-glazed variants should be avoided as they introduce added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) offset by very high sodium content (1015mg/100g) which elevates cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While smoked salmon provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (approximately 2.4g polyunsaturated fat per 100g) and lean protein, the very high sodium content (1015mg/100g) poses a significant cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portions to 20–30g and prefer fresh or unsalted salmon to obtain omega-3 benefits without the sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 1015mg/100g far exceeds the 400mg RA threshold, potentially worsening inflammation and tissue swelling despite beneficial omega-3 content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Despite being an excellent source of anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids, smoked salmon is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its very high sodium content (1015mg/100g); fresh or frozen unsalted salmon is strongly preferred to obtain omega-3 benefits without exacerbating RA-related inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Smoked salmon is a processed fish product made from salmon fillets or slices that have been cured with salt and then preserved through a smoking process, most commonly cold-smoking at temperatures below 30°C to maintain a silky, tender texture without fully cooking the fish. The result is a product with a distinctive rich, savoury, mildly smoky flavour and a soft, slightly oily texture that separates into thin, translucent slices. Smoked salmon is widely consumed across Western and international cuisines, particularly in Scandinavian, Jewish-American, and British culinary traditions, where it is served on bagels with cream cheese, on blinis, in salads, or as part of charcuterie and seafood platters. Nutritionally, smoked salmon is a high-protein food (24.3g/100g) with a moderate fat content (10.1g/100g) that includes beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. It contains zero carbohydrates and zero sugars, making it glycaemically neutral and suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. However, the sodium content is very high at 1015mg/100g due to the curing process, which makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, or those on sodium-restricted diets. Clinically, smoked salmon is an excellent protein source for Type 2 Diabetes management but must be consumed in small portions by those managing blood pressure or cardiovascular risk. Per 100g it provides 188 kcal, 24.3g protein, 10.1g fat, 0g carbohydrate, and 1015mg sodium.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in smoked salmon?
Is smoked salmon good for weight loss?
Can I eat smoked salmon if I have high blood pressure?
What are the omega-3 benefits of smoked salmon?
How many calories in smoked salmon?
Is smoked salmon raw or cooked?
What is the difference between lox and smoked salmon?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F03040012, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved regional source per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and potassium values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15236, smoked Atlantic salmon), which is an approved secondary global source. Both sources are explicitly listed in the approved source list. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a processed smoked fish product.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F03040012) — smoked salmon contains no carbohydrates by its natural composition and processing method (confirmed zero). Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (confirmed zero). Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as smoked salmon is an animal-derived fish product containing no plant fibre (naturally zero). Trans_fat_g is 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15236 cross-reference for smoked Atlantic salmon (confirmed zero). Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB smoked salmon entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16; these values were not incorporated from USDA SR Legacy to preserve primary source integrity with the HPB canonical record. Potassium_mg (330mg) and cholesterol_mg (23mg) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15236 as the HPB entry does not report these values; both are approved secondary source values. Saturated_fat_g (2.3g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.7g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.4g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15236 as the HPB entry does not report fatty acid breakdown. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per the critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (smoked fish) is a broader closest_parent match as no species-specific smoked salmon code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Smoked salmon is a Western/International food product; no mandatory local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules as it is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Smoked Salmon', a precise canonical English food name. Smoked salmon is a Western/International food product; no mandatory local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (188.0), protein_g (24.3), fat_g (10.1), and carbohydrate_g (0.0) are all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (CR ID F03040012), an approved primary source. Carbohydrate_g zero is justified in notes as confirmed zero per HPB source. nutrient_source explicitly names both HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved sources with direct URLs. G2 approved-source compliance is satisfied.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (1015.0), saturated_fat_g (2.3), and sugar_g (0.0) are all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and supported by HPB primary source and USDA SR Legacy cross-reference.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is null (not 0, satisfying the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods), gi_category is 'not_applicable', glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0, and gi_source documents the not-applicable rationale with source_match_type 'not_applicable'. Smoked salmon contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g confirmed by HPB. GI is not applicable per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods including all fish and seafood with negligible carbohydrates.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03302114 (smoked salmon food product) is a defensible exact match. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (smoked fish) is a defensible closest_parent match with gap note explaining no species-specific code exists. SNOMED CT is null with honest gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters), explains what smoked salmon is, how it is prepared (cold-smoking after salt curing), taste and texture, cultural context (Scandinavian, Jewish-American, British traditions), clinical relevance (hypertension, diabetes, cardiovascular, RA), and key nutrients (protein, fat, omega-3, sodium, energy).

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium 1015mg/100g), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, zero carbohydrate), and Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, omega-3 benefit offset by high sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' is correctly used for the type2_diabetes key. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis is included in additional_conditions.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 9 entries covering canonical name, raw input name, preparation variants (cold-smoked, lox, gravlax-style, cured), species variants (Atlantic, Pacific), and French romanized form. aliases_by_language is populated for English and romanized. Self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases array — 'Smoked Salmon', 'Smoked salmon, raw', 'Cold-smoked salmon', 'Lox', 'Gravlax-style smoked salmon', 'Cured smoked salmon', 'Smoked Atlantic salmon', 'Smoked Pacific salmon' all in english array; 'Salmon fumé' in romanized array — all confirmed present in flat aliases. Q8 consistency check passed.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Fixed G2 approved-source compliance failure by restructuring nutrient_source to explicitly name both HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved sources with their respective URLs and organization names, making dual-source provenance unambiguous to the validator.

Iteration 3: Fixed Q4 glycemic completeness failure by changing gi_source.source_match_type from 'exact' to 'not_applicable' to accurately reflect that no GI database lookup was performed, and expanded gi_source.title and source_match_notes to clearly document the not-applicable rationale per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

Iteration 3: Confirmed glycemic_index remains null (not numeric 0) per the critical rule — this was already correct in iteration 2 but the validator flagged it as 'must be numeric'; null is the correct value for not-applicable GI per the critical rule which states 'Set glycemic_index: null (NOT 0)'.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type to 'closest_parent' for FoodEx2 code A0CKP.

Iteration 1: Added multilingual local_names and aliases_by_language structure, added health_context for all three required conditions including Type 2 Diabetes, expanded natural_language_description to exceed 200 characters, added ontology anchors.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Smoked Salmon (F03040012) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. Previous REJECT issues from iteration 2 have been resolved: G2 approved-source compliance is now satisfied by explicitly naming both HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved sources in nutrient_source with dual organization names and URLs; Q4 glycemic completeness is now satisfied with gi_source.source_match_type set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index correctly set to null per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. All other criteria remain passing from iteration 2.
